commonIssues: Bolting (premature flowering) in summer heat and long days; provide light afternoon shade and consistent moisture to delay it. Sources confirm bolting is triggered by heat and by irregular watering / dry soil.
Sensitive to both frost and heat; not cold hardy below freezing and prone to stress in hot weather. Protect from frost and avoid midday sun in hot climates.
Containers dry out faster than the ground (RHS): check daily in warm weather and keep compost just moist without waterlogging.
Avoid over-fertilizing; only light nitrogen feeding is needed (Clemson).
Sow successively every 1-3 weeks for a continuous leaf supply, as individual plants are short-lived.

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - The Cilantro-Coriander Connection: at least six hours of sun per day; soil pH 6-7 with 20-25% organic matter, well-drained; about an inch of water a week once established; spacing 6-8 in apart; light nitrogen fertilizer, avoid over-fertilizing; bolts as temperatures rise late in the season.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - The Cilantro-Coriander Connection: about an inch of water a week once established unless extremely hot; well-drained soil; bolts when temperatures rise.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - The Cilantro-Coriander Connection: light applications of a nitrogen fertilizer keep plants lush; avoid the tendency to over-fertilize. Soil pH 6-7 with 20-25% organic matter, well-drained.

RHS - How to grow Coriander: thrives in a sunny or lightly shaded spot; for summer leaf harvests use cooler/midday-shaded positions to prevent early flowering (bolting); performs particularly well in containers due to free-draining preference; container plants dry faster - check daily and keep compost just moist without waterlogging; space ~10cm (4in) apart; bolts in hot, dry weather; harvest leaves at 10-15cm (4-6in); seeds ~10+ weeks from sowing.

RHS - How to grow Coriander: container-grown coriander should be checked daily and watered to keep compost just moist; ground-grown needs minimal watering except in extended dry periods; bolts in hot, dry weather, regular watering delays flowering.

UC Master Gardener Program of Sonoma County - Cilantro and Coriander: prefers full sun except in hot microclimates where light shade is preferred; water soil regularly because irregular watering and dry soil cause bolting; cilantro is neither frost- nor heat-tolerant; spring planting from about May 1, succession sow every 1-3 weeks; thin to 3-4 in apart; small crops thrive in containers; fall planting needs at least 60 days for leafy growth before first frost.
UC Master Gardener Program of Sonoma County - Cilantro and Coriander: cilantro is neither frost- nor heat-tolerant; plant in spring after last frost and again in fall; succession sow every 1-3 weeks.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Coriandrum sativum: full sun to part shade, light afternoon shade helps avoid sun scald in hot summer climates; medium water needs in well-drained soils; USDA zones 2-11; mature height 1.5-2 ft; leaf pinching can begin in 30 days, seeds ready in 90 days; plants bolt easily in hot summer climates.

The Old Farmer's Almanac - Coriander and Cilantro: plant in spring after the last frost date or in fall; not cold hardy and requires protection from frost; plants prefer at least 6 hours of sun, but for leaf production partial shade is more productive; plants bolt in summer heat; sow a new patch every 2-3 weeks for continuous supply.

Confidence note: Exact coldHardyMinC and heatStressC are not published as numeric thresholds by the cited extension/RHS/Almanac sources. Sources uniformly state cilantro is frost-sensitive (not cold hardy) and bolts in summer heat. coldHardyMinC set to 0C (freezing) as a conservative protect-from-frost floor; heatStressC set to 27C (~80F) as a conservative bolting-onset estimate consistent with 'bolts as temperatures rise in summer'. Treat both as estimates.
